Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Defendant Ideo, L.P.S Deposition Subpoena For Production Of Consumer Records (Marin Health)
Off Calendar Jul-31-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF DIANA LYMAN's Motion To Quash Defendant Ideo, L.P.S Deposition Subpoena For Production Of Consumer Records (Marin Health):

Judge pro tem Maxwell Platt's well-reasoned report and recommendation is adopted. Plaintiff Diana Lyman's motion to quash defendant IDEO, LP's deposition subpoena to Marin Health is granted in part and denied in part. The sanctions requests are denied.

No response to the subpoena has been provided, so no separate statement was required. (CRC 3.1345(b)(1).) Plaintiff does not dispute that she waived privacy rights to records pertinent to her claims. But it is unclear what non-pertinent records, if any, exist. In any event, a protective order can protect plaintiff's privacy rights. (See Schnabel v. Sup. Ct. (1993) 5 Cal.4th 704, 718.) Counsel are ordered to meet and confer and then file a stipulated protective order if they can. If they cannot so stipulate, plaintiff may move for a protective order. Defendant's waiver argument is premature, as plaintiff has yet to assert privilege to specific records or information. If plaintiff does assert privilege after reviewing documents, she shall provide a privilege log to defendant within 14 days of plaintiff's receipt of the documents.

order signed.

Notice Of Motion And Motion Of Defendant Ideo Lp To Strike Portions Of Plaintiffs Complaint
Off Calendar Jun-04-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES: Atty. RACHEL S. BRAVO, Esq. of Nichols Law Firm (Tel: 415-504-3095) appearing via CourtCall for the plaintiff;
Atty. SEAN R. BRODERICK, Esq. of Simpson Garrity Innes & Jacuzzi (Tel: 650-615-4860) appearing via CourtCall for defendant IDEO LP.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT IDEO LP, ERRONEOUSLY SUED HEREIN AS "IDEO, L.P."'S MOTION TO STRIKE PORTIONS OF PLAINTIFFS COMPLAINT.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows:

Defendant's motion to strike Plaintiff's allegations and prayer for punitive damages is denied. "Section 3294 permits punitive damages against a corporate employer if the employee is sufficiently high in the corporation's decision-making hierarchy to be an 'officer, director or managing agent.'" (Gelfo v. Lockheed Martin Corp. (2006) 140 Cal.App.4th 34, 63.) According to the complaint, Mr. Aycan was "Managing Director of IDEO Products." (Compl. 3.)

Punitive damages are recoverable for FEHA violations. (Commodore Home Systems, Inc. v. Superior Court (1982) 32 Cal.3d 211, 221.) Defendant does not show that plaintiff's allegations fail to rise to the requisite level of fraud, malice, or oppression. It is true that "wrongful termination, without more, will not sustain a finding of malice or oppression." (Scott v. Phoenix Sch., Inc. (2009) 175 Cal.App.4th 702, 717.) Here, however, plaintiff alleges that she was discriminated against on the basis of her gender and pregnancy, which can provide a basis for recovery of punitive damages. (See Cloud v. Casey (1999) 76 Cal.App.4th 895, 912 ["The jury could properly conclude that the [defendant] intentionally discriminated by denying [plaintiff] a promotion based on gender, then attempted to hide the illegal reason for their decision with a false explanation, and that in this, they acted in a manner that was base, contemptible or vile. The discriminatory employment decision denied [plaintiff] her protected right under FEHA to seek, obtain, and hold employment without discrimination or abridgment on account of sex."]; see also, e.g., Monge v. Superior Court (1986) 176 Cal.App.3d 503, 511.)

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria A. Torreano CSR#8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/EPS)

DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jun-04-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES: Atty. RACHEL S. BRAVO, Esq. of Nichols Law Firm (Tel: 415-504-3095) appearing via CourtCall for the plaintiff;
Atty. SEAN R. BRODERICK, Esq. of Simpson Garrity Innes & Jacuzzi (Tel: 650-615-4860) appearing via CourtCall for defendant IDEO LP.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT IDEO LP, ERRONEOUSLY SUED HEREIN AS "IDEO, L.P."'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows:

Defendant's demurrer is overruled in its entirety.

Defendant's demurrer for uncertainty is overruled. A "demurrer for uncertainty is strictly construed, even where a complaint is in some respects uncertain, because ambiguities can be clarified under modern discovery procedures." (Khoury v. Maly's of California (1993) 14 Cal.App.4th 612, 616; see also Lickiss v. Financial Industry Regulatory Authority (2012) 208 Cal.App.4th 1125, 1135 ["demurrers for uncertainty are disfavored, and are granted only if the pleading is so incomprehensible that a defendant cannot reasonably respond."].) Defendant makes no legitimate argument, nor does it provide any support, that the causes of action are uncertain because there are allegations that could support an alternative theory. While the Rules of Court require identification of causes of action, "the lack of labels for plaintiff's causes of action does not substantially impair [the defendant's] ability to understand the complaint?." (Williams v. Beechnut Nutrition Corp. (1986) 185 Cal.App.3d 135, 139, fn. 2.)

Defendant's demurrer for failure to state a cause of action is overruled. Defendant's argument is devoid of any legal authority or support. Defendant fails to show that the first and second causes of action for discrimination are inadequately pled. California law does not require a plaintiff to allege with specificity "how, when[,] and where" she was treated differently "because of any protected characteristic." (Def.'s Dem 13:10.) Rather, California follows the McDonnell Douglas burden shifting test which "reflects the principle that direct evidence of intentional discrimination is rare." Instead the plaintiff only needs to allege, and provide evidence of, "some other circumstance [that] suggest[] discriminatory motive." (Guz v. Bechtel Nat. Inc. (2000) 24 Cal.4th 317, 355.) Plaintiff's allegations that she was discriminated against on the basis of her gender and pregnancy are adequate. (See, e.g., Compl. pars. 6, 8, 9-10, 34-37, 45-48.) So are her claims for disability discrimination/ failure to provide reasonable accommodation and to engage in the interactive process. (See, e.g., Compl. pars. 12-15, 21-22, 55-57. 64-68.)

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria A. Torreano CSR#8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/EPS)
